intro.title: Introduction
intro.p1: Components Library is an open-source collection of carefully designed UI components that aim to enhance your React web applications.
intro.p2: This is not your typical component library, which means you won't find a set of generic buttons, inputs, or other common UI elements here.
intro.p3: Basically, these components are here to help you stand out and make a statement visually by adding a touch of creativity to your projects.
intro.missionP1: The goal of Components Library is simple - provide flexible, visually stunning and most importantly, free components that take web projects to the next level.
intro.missionPrinciples: To make that happen, the project is committed to the following principles:
intro.freeForAllDesc: You own the code, and it's free to use in your projects
intro.propFirstDesc: Easy customization through thoughtfully exposed props
intro.fullyModularDesc: Install strictly what you need, Components Library is not a dependency
intro.freeChoiceDesc: JS or TS, plain CSS or Tailwind, the code is all here
intro.freeForAllSection: Every component you choose to bring into your project is yours to modify or extend, because you get full visibility of the code, not just an import.
intro.propFirstSection: Every component is designed to be flexible and customizable, with props that allow you to adjust the look and feel without having to always dive into the code.
intro.fullyModularSection: Components Library is not your classic NPM library, you install only the components you need by either copying the code or using the CLI, without pulling in a whole library.
intro.freeChoiceSection: I don't want to dictate how you build your projects. Whether you prefer JavaScript or TypeScript, plain CSS or Tailwind, it's all here for you to use as you see fit.
intro.ps: P.S. The header has a neat dropdown to help you choose your preferred technologies.
intro.performanceDesc: While we do everything possible to optimize components and offer the best experience, here are some tips to keep in mind when using Components Library:
intro.lessIsMoreDesc: Using more than 2-3 components on a page is not advised, it can overload your page with animations, potentially impacting performance or UX
intro.mobileOptimizationDesc: Consider disabling certain effects on mobile and replacing them with static placeholders instead
intro.testThoroughlyDesc: Your device may be high-end, but be considerate of your users - always test on multiple devices before going live

install.title: Installation
install.desc: Using components is very straightforward, anyone can do it.
install.methodDesc: You can keep it simple and copy code directly from the documentation, or you can use CLI commands to install components into your project.
install.clickCards: Click the cards below to change your preferred method.

manual.desc: Follow these steps to manually install components:
manual.step1Desc: Preview components and find something you like, then head to the Code tab.
manual.step2Desc: Components may use external libraries, don't forget to install them by selecting Manual, copying the command, and running it in your terminal.
manual.step3Desc: The Code tab also contains all the code you need to copy - you can use the controls below to switch between technologies on the Code tab.
manual.step4Desc: A basic usage example is provided for every component, and if you want to go into details, you can check all the available props on the Preview tab.

cli.desc: Use a one-time command to pull any component directly into your project.
cli.intro: Components Library supports two CLI installation methods: shadcn and jsrepo. Pick whichever you prefer – they both fetch the same component source.
cli.installDesc: Below are example commands for the SplitText component. Replace placeholders to fit your stack.
cli.tip: Tip: You can run these with other package managers (pnpm, yarn, bun) - just swap the prefix (e.g. pnpm dlx or yarn instead of npx).

install.thatsAllDesc: From here on, it's all about how you integrate the component into your project. The code is yours to play around with - modify styling, functionalities, anything goes!

mcp.title: MCP Server
mcp.intro: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that enables AI assistants to securely connect to external data sources and tools.
mcp.introDesc: Components Library encourages the use of the shadcn MCP server to browse, search, and install components using natural language.
mcp.registriesDesc: Registries are configured in your project's components.json file, where you should first add the @components-library registry:
mcp.setupDesc: Then, from the options below, select your client & set up the shadcn MCP server.
mcp.claudeRestart: Restart Claude Code and try prompts like:
mcp.claudeTip: Tip: Use /mcp in Claude Code to debug the MCP server.
mcp.cursorEnable: Then open Cursor Settings and enable the shadcn MCP server. Try prompts like:
mcp.vscodeEnable: Then open .vscode/mcp.json and click Start next to the shadcn server. Try prompts like:
mcp.officialDocs: To learn more about using the shadcn MCP server, including manual setup for different clients, please visit the official documentation:
prompts: Show me all the available backgrounds from the Components Library registry
Add the Dither background from Components Library to the page, make it purple
Add a new section which fades in on scroll using FadeContent from Components Library

tools.title: Interactive Tools
tools.subtitle: Interactive tools to help you build and customize your projects faster than ever.
tools.settingsSoon: Settings for this tool will be available soon.

background-studio.label: Background Studio
background-studio.description: Explore animated backgrounds for your projects. Choose from various effects and customize as you like. Export as video, image, or code or share your creations as URLs.

shape-magic.label: Shape Magic
shape-magic.description: Tool for automagically creating inner rounded corners between shapes of different sizes. Export as code or SVG.

texture-lab.label: Texture Lab
texture-lab.description: Apply effects to your images and export the results. Add noise, dithering, halftone, ASCII art, and more. Save your presets for sharing or future use.
